architecture.summary: This is a survey paper on Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG), a paradigm that augments large language models with structured knowledge retrieval. GraphRAG structures domain knowledge as graphs (either as explicit knowledge carriers or indexing tools) and retrieves relevant subgraphs to augment LLM generation. The system flow is: (1) Knowledge Organization—convert unstructured text into graph-structured representations or use graphs to index text chunks; (2) Knowledge Retrieval—use graph-based planners (learnable or algorithm-based) to retrieve query-relevant subgraphs; (3) Knowledge Integration—incorporate retrieved subgraph information into the LLM prompt. GraphRAG addresses traditional RAG limitations in complex query understanding, distributed knowledge integration, context window constraints, and scalability by enabling multi-hop reasoning and pruning irrelevant information through structured graph navigation.

biblio.title: A Survey of Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Customized Large Language Models

claims.statement: Traditional RAG systems based on flat text retrieval face three critical challenges: (i) complex query understanding in professional contexts, (ii) difficulties in knowledge integration across distributed sources, and (iii) system efficiency bottlenecks at scale

GraphRAG addresses traditional RAG limitations through three key innovations: (i) graph-structured knowledge representation that explicitly captures entity relationships and domain hierarchies, (ii) efficient graph-based retrieval techniques that enable context-preserving knowledge retrieval with multihop reasoning ability, and (iii) structure-aware knowledge integration algorithms that leverage retrieved knowledge for accurate and logical coherent generation

Traditional RAG systems that rely on simple keyword matching and vector similarity techniques are inadequate for capturing the deep semantic nuances and multi-step reasoning processes necessary for accurate and comprehensive understanding. When queried about the connection between concept A and concept D, these systems typically retrieve only directly related information, missing crucial intermediate concepts like B and C that could bridge the relationship

Domain knowledge is usually collected from different sources with varying levels of quality, accuracy, and completeness. The retrieved knowledge is often flattened, extensive, and intricate, while domain concepts are typically scattered across multiple documents without clear hierarchical relationships

LLMs are constrained by their fixed context window (typically 2K-32K tokens). Long-range dependencies in complex documents cannot be fully captured, as content exceeding the context window must be truncated or summarized, disrupting natural semantic units and logical flow

Existing GraphRAG models can be categorized into three main categories: (1) Knowledge-based GraphRAG, which uses graphs as knowledge carriers; (2) Index-based GraphRAG which uses graphs as index tools to retrieve relevant raw texts from the corpus; and (3) Hybrid GraphRAG which combines the strengths of both knowledge-based and index-based frameworks

Knowledge-based GraphRAG focuses on transforming unstructured textual documents into explicit and structured knowledge graphs, where nodes represent domain concepts and edges capture semantic relationships. Index-based GraphRAG maintains the original textual form while utilizing graph structures primarily as an indexing mechanism to organize and retrieve relevant text chunks efficiently

GraphRAG utilizes graph structures to represent knowledge, capturing complex relationships between entities and concepts, allowing for a more nuanced and contextual understanding of information compared to the flat document-based representation used in traditional RAG. The graph structure can represent hierarchies, associations, and multi-hop relationships, providing a richer semantic context for queries
